返回本批次

阿耳忒弥斯二号升空：四名宇航员开始为期 10 天的月球任务

Artemis II lifts off: four astronauts begin 10-day lunar mission

www.theguardian.com 作者 Philpax 分数 275 评论 1 2026/04/02 06:38
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 3 小时前阿耳忒弥斯二号升空：四名宇航员开始为期 10 天的月球任务美国宇航局发射了阿耳忒弥斯二号，执行历史性的载人登月任务。这次为期 10 天的试飞虽然不会登陆月球，但却是一项充满里程碑意义的任务。这次任务包括第一位飞入地月空间（地球轨道和月球之间的区域）的女性和第一个有色人种。阿耳忒弥斯二号的猎户座太空舱可以让她们比之前的任何人类都离地球更远。关键事件2小时前结束总结3小时前阿耳忒弥斯二号进入地球轨道3小时前阿耳忒弥斯二号升空：四名宇航员开始为期10天的月球任务3小时前开始发射！新时间 6.35pm ET4 小时前发射延迟！4 小时前电池问题“不会影响发射”4 小时前工程师评估电池问题5 小时前安全系统问题已解决，倒计时继续5 小时前未来5 小时前阿尔忒弥斯工程师工作安全系统问题6 小时前特朗普欢呼“我们勇敢的宇航员”，冷落加拿大人6 小时前月球任务历史7 小时前阿尔忒弥斯二号机组人员坐在猎户座太空舱中7 小时前做什么了解航天器 8 小时前阿尔忒弥斯 II 任务 8 小时前阿尔忒弥斯 II 机组人员穿着宇航服的第一张照片 8 小时前谁是阿尔忒弥斯 II 机组人员8 小时前如何观看阿耳忒弥斯 II 任务8 小时前预计发射将如何展开仅显示关键事件请打开 JavaScript 以使用此功能结束语总结在观看了太空火箭从肯尼迪航天中心发射到佛罗里达蔚蓝天空的壮观场面后，我们现在将关闭有关阿尔忒弥斯 II 发射的实时博客。四名宇航员——美国人里德·怀斯曼、维克多·格洛弗和克里斯蒂娜·科赫，以及加拿大航天局的杰里米·汉森，于下午 6 点 35 分从 39B 发射台升空，踏上前往月球的途中。他们为期 10 天的月球飞越是半个多世纪以来首次载人登月任务。自 1972 年 12 月阿波罗 17 号以来，没有其他人类曾飞越近地轨道。阿耳忒弥斯 II 是一次试飞，旨在在未来的阿耳忒弥斯任务之前评估猎户座乘员舱以及必要的生命支持和医疗系统，包括计划于 2028 年阿耳忒弥斯 IV 进行的下一次登月。感谢您关注我们的发射，请继续关注我们的任务新闻以及阿耳忒弥斯 II 乘员 10 天内在太平洋溅落的报道以下是一些聚集在卡纳维拉尔角附近观看阿耳忒弥斯二号宇航员在佛罗里达州晚上开始历史性登月通勤的地球人的照片。2026 年 4 月 1 日，人们在佛罗里达州泰特斯维尔的 A. Max Brewer 桥上观看阿耳忒弥斯二号的发射。照片：Gerardo Mora/Getty Images 观众观看阿耳忒弥斯二号火箭升空。照片：Marco Bello/Reuters 美国宇航局阿耳忒弥斯二号机组人员升空，旁观者捕捉到了卡纳维拉尔角的这一瞬间。摄影：Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images 4 月 1 日，一名初级宇航员在阿耳忒弥斯二号发射场附近做手势。照片：Marco Bello/Reuters 当美国宇航局阿耳忒弥斯二号机组人员从卡纳维拉尔角起飞时，人们欢呼雀跃。照片：Marco Bello/Reuters 今晚壮观的发射的高级观看点很快就挤满了人。卡纳维拉尔角、泰特斯维尔和可可海滩等佛罗里达州太空海岸城市的官员表示，他们预计海滩和堤道上将有多达 40 万观众。周三早上 7 点前不久，黎明时分，数十辆汽车就停在泰特斯维尔的海滨，泰特斯维尔自称是“通往太空和太空的门户”。周三，观众在佛罗里达州泰特斯维尔的太空景观公园占据了观看阿耳忒弥斯二号发射的有利位置。照片：Marco Bello/Reuters 从这座城市可以直接看到印度河对面的 39B 发射台，那里的人群让人想起 1960 年代和 70 年代的阿波罗时代，当时数百万人涌入观看首次登月任务。泰特斯维尔市长安德鲁·康纳斯 (Andrew Connors) 在上周接受采访时告诉我：“肯尼迪航天中心有 3 条入口，其中两条穿过城市。” II 号将带来可喜的意外之财，但康纳斯也有点担心。“想想这真是太疯狂了，因为我们是一座拥有 51,000 人的城市，”他说。“所有的桥梁都很快就被填满了，我确信主要路线将是一个停车场，但我们的警察已经这样做了很多次。这真的很特别。”阅读泰特斯维尔市长和其他太空海岸人物的更多信息，请点击这里：现在与猎户座一起从佛罗里达州肯尼迪航天中心壮观而完美地升空后，绕地球飞行半个多小时后，地面任务管理人员正在评估数据。休斯顿的飞行控制人员已确认所有四个太阳能电池阵列均已成功部署。美国宇航局领导人无疑会自豪地微笑着，将于东部时间晚上 9 点举行发射后新闻发布会。届时我们的博客将关闭，但随着为期 10 天的阿耳忒弥斯二号任务的其余部分的展开，《卫报》将继续为您带来新闻。贾里德·艾萨克曼，美国宇航局管理员在美国宇航局电视台上谈到了阿耳忒弥斯二号的发射。他说，“这是猎户座飞船的开场表演，测试任务。”“没有人类曾经飞过这艘飞船。我们正在测试它的速度，以确保它没问题。它将建立后续任务[以及]科学和发现的黄金时代。” “点火后，我最兴奋的时刻是溅落，”他说，“最大的收获是在猎户座飞船中获得额外的舒适感。这对我们来说是一个新领域。SLS 加上猎户座就是一切。在这一次，我们希望确保我们以尽可能安全的方式做到这一点。”在猎户座太空舱内，宇航员里德·怀斯曼、维克多·格洛弗、克里斯蒂娜·科赫和杰里米·汉森提出了他们的要求护目镜，并立即开始评估航天器如何处理以 17,500 英里/小时的速度上升到轨道的任务。太阳能电池阵列翼的部署即将开始，这将为猎户座在整个月球旅程中提供持续的电力。阿尔忒弥斯 II 进入地球轨道阿尔忒弥斯 II 现已在地球轨道上。太空发射系统的两个固体火箭助推器已经分离，正在飘回大西洋进行回收。航天器将绕地球运行，直到飞行第二天（星期四），届时将进行跨月喷射燃烧，并将其送上剩下的 240,000 英里的月球旅程。真是令人兴奋。阿耳忒弥斯二号的成功发射看起来非常酷。美国宇航局的阿耳忒弥斯二号飞越月球任务从佛罗里达州卡纳维拉尔角的肯尼迪航天中心升空。照片：Joe Skipper/Reuters 阿耳忒弥斯二号升空：四名宇航员开始为期 10 天的月球任务 美国宇航局发射了阿耳忒弥斯二号，执行一项历史性的载人登月任务。这次为期 10 天的试飞虽然不会登陆月球，但却是一项充满里程碑意义的任务。该任务包括第一位飞入月地空间（地球轨道和月球之间的区域）的女性和有色人种。阿耳忒弥斯二号的猎户座太空舱可以让她们比之前的任何人类都离地球更远。出发吧！新时间美国东部时间下午 6 点 35 分，任务管理人员的投票已经结束，正式名称为发射准备情况检查。结论是“发射团队此时已准备好继续进行”。现在是发射总监查理·布莱克韦尔-汤普森进行的最终投票。一切都很好。她说：双引号里德、维克多、克里斯蒂娜和杰里米，在这一历史性使命中，你们承载着阿尔忒弥斯团队的心，承载着美国人民和我们全球合作伙伴的大胆精神，承载着新一代的希望和梦想。祝你好运，上帝保佑阿尔忒弥斯二世。我们走吧。倒计时已恢复，10 分钟后于美国东部时间下午 6.35 分（英国夏令时晚上 11.35 分）起飞。发射延迟！任务管理人员已宣布他们正在解决一些问题，这些问题将推迟今晚阿耳忒弥斯 II 的发射，原定于美国东部时间下午 6.24 分进行。发射总监查理·布莱克韦尔-汤普森表示，该建议仍将在某个时间点发射，但我们还不知道可能会提供什么新时间。最终发射和地面团队即将进行民意调查，以确定今晚的发射是否会进行。待命……电池问题“不会影响发射”美国宇航局阿耳特弥斯二号博客的欢迎电池更新：双引号工程师调查了发射中止系统姿态控制电机控制器电池上的传感器，该传感器显示出比预期更高的温度。据信这是仪器问题，不会影响今天的发射。我们又回来了。再次。

Original Summary

  1. From 3h agoArtemis II lifts off: four astronauts begin 10-day lunar missionNasa launched Artemis II on a historic crewed mission to the moon.The 10-day test flight, which will not land on the moon, is a mission packed with milestones. The mission includes the first woman and first person of color to fly into cislunar space, the area between Earth’s orbit and the moon.Artemis II’s Orion space capsule could fly them farther from Earth than any human being before them.Key events2h agoClosing summary3h agoArtemis II enters Earth's orbit3h agoArtemis II lifts off: four astronauts begin 10-day lunar mission3h agoGo for launch! New time 6.35pm ET4h agoLaunch delay!4h agoBattery issue 'won't affect launch'4h agoEngineers evaluating battery issue5h agoSafety system issue resolved, countdown continues5h agoThe future5h agoArtemis engineers working safety system issue6h agoTrump hails "our brave astronauts", snubs Canadian6h agoMoon mission history7h agoArtemis II crew seated in Orion capsule7h agoWhat to know about the spacecraft8h agoThe Artemis II mission8h agoFirst photos of Artemis II crew in their space suits8h agoWho is on the Artemis II crew8h agoHow to watch the Artemis II mission8h agoHow the launch is expected to unfoldShow key events onlyPlease turn on JavaScript to use this featureClosing summaryWe’re closing our live blog of the launch of Artemis II now after watching the space rocket’s spectacular launch into a clear blue Florida sky from the Kennedy Space Center. Four astronauts, Americans Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover and Christina Koch, plus Jeremy Hansen from the Canadian Space Agency, are on their way to the moon after lifting off at 6.35pm from launchpad 39B. Their 10-day lunar flyby is the first crewed mission to the moon in more than half a century. No other humans have traveled beyond lower Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972. Artemis II is a test flight designed to evaluate the Orion crew capsule and essential life support and medical systems ahead of future Artemis missions, including the next moon landing scheduled for Artemis IV in 2028. Thank you for following the launch with us, and stick with us for news of the mission and coverage of the Artemis II crew’s splashdown in the Pacific Ocean in 10 days’ time.Here’s a look at some of the earthlings who gathered near Cape Canaveral to watch the Artemis II crew begin their historic lunar commute on this Florida evening.People observe the launch of Artemis II from the A. Max Brewer Bridge in Titusville, Florida on 1 April 2026. Photograph: Gerardo Mora/Getty ImagesSpectators watch as the Artemis II rocket lifts off. Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersNasa’s Artemis II crew blasts off as onlookers capture the moment in Cape Canaveral. Photograph: Chip Somodevilla/Getty ImagesA junior astronaut gestures near the launch site of the Artemis II on 1 April. Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersPeople cheer as Nasa’s Artemis II crew blasts off from Cape Canaveral. Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersPremium viewing spots for tonight’s spectacular launch filled up fast.Officials in Florida’s space coast cities, including Cape Canaveral, Titusville, and Cocoa Beach, said they were expecting up to 400,000 spectators to fill beaches and causeways.As early as first light, shortly before 7am on Wednesday, dozens of cars were already parked along the waterfront in Titusville, which bills itself as “the gateway to space and nature”.Spectators secure a vantage spot for the Artemis II launch at Space View Park, Titusville, Florida, on Wednesday. Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersThe city has a direct view across the Indian river to launchpad 39B, and the crowds there are a reminder of the Apollo era of the 1960s and 70s when millions packed in to watch the first moon missions.“There’s three entry ways to the Kennedy Space Center and two of them go through the city,” Andrew Connors, the mayor of Titusville, told me in an interview last week.An influx of hundreds of thousands for Artemis II will bring a welcome financial windfall, but Connors is also a little apprehensive.“It’s pretty crazy to think about it because we’re a city of 51,000,” he said.“All the bridges fill up really quickly and I’m sure the main route through will be a parking lot, but our police have been doing this a lot of times. It’s something really special.”Read more from the Titusville mayor, and other space coast figures, here:With Orion now orbiting Earth, a little more than half an hour into flight after a spectacular and flawless lift-off from Florida’s Kennedy Space Center, mission managers on the ground are assessing data.Flight controllers in Houston have confirmed that all four solar arrays were deployed successfully.Nasa leaders, no doubt beaming with pride, will conduct a post-launch press conference scheduled for 9pm ET. Our blog will have closed by then, but the Guardian will continue to bring you news as the rest of the 10-day Artemis II mission unfolds.Jared Isaacman, the Nasa administrator, spoke about the Artemis II launch on Nasa TV.“It’s the opening act, the test mission,” for the Orion spacecraft, he said.“No humans have ever flown on this. We’re putting it through its paces to make sure it’s OK. It’s going to set up subsequent missions [and] a golden age of science and discovery.”Isaacman, a billionaire private astronaut and Donald Trump’s pick to lead the agency, who was confirmed earlier this year, was asked what his favorite moment of the mission would be.“After ignition, the moment I’m most excited for is splashdown,” he said.“The takeaway is gaining extra comfort in the Orion spacecraft. It’s new territory for us. SLS plus Orion is everything. On this one we want to make sure we do this in as safe a way as we can.”Inside the Orion capsule, astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen have raised their visors and are immediately commencing tasks to assess how the spacecraft handled the 17,500mph ascent to orbit.Deployment of the solar array wings, which will provide Orion with continuous electrical power throughout its lunar journey, is about to begin.Artemis II enters Earth's orbitArtemis II is now in Earth’s orbit. The two solid rocket boosters of the Space Launch System have separated and are floating back down to the Atlantic for recovery.The spacecraft will orbit Earth until flight day two (Thursday) when the translunar injection burn will take place and sent it on the rest of its 240,000-mile journey to the moon.What a thrill. Artemis II’s successful launch looked incredibly cool.NASA's Artemis II mission to fly by the moon lifts off from the Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Florida. Photograph: Joe Skipper/ReutersArtemis II lifts off: four astronauts begin 10-day lunar missionNasa launched Artemis II on a historic crewed mission to the moon.The 10-day test flight, which will not land on the moon, is a mission packed with milestones. The mission includes the first woman and first person of color to fly into cislunar space, the area between Earth’s orbit and the moon.Artemis II’s Orion space capsule could fly them farther from Earth than any human being before them.Go for launch! New time 6.35pm ETPolling of mission managers has concluded, officially known as the launch readiness check.The verdict is “launch teams are ready to proceed at this time”.Now it’s the final poll conducted by launch director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson. Things are good.She said: double quotation markReid, Victor, Christina and Jeremy, on this historic mission you take the heart of this Artemis team, the daring spirit of of the American people and our partners across the globe, and the hopes and dreams of a new generation. Good luck, God speed Artemis II. Let’s go. The countdown clock has resumed, lift-off in 10 minutes, at 6.35pm ET (11.35pm BST)Launch delay!Mission managers have announced they are working a few issues that will delay tonight’s Artemis II launch from its original 6.24pm ET time.Launch director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson says the recommendation is still to launch at some point, but we don’t yet know what new time might be provided.Final polls of launch and ground teams are about to take place to see if tonight’s launch will happen or not. Stand by…Battery issue 'won't affect launch'A welcome battery update from Nasa’s Artemis II blog: double quotation markEngineers investigated a sensor on the launch abort system’s attitude control motor controller battery that showed a higher temperature than would be expected. It is believed to be an instrumentation issue and will not affect today’s launch. We are back on. Again.
